informal: Prove that if $P \in \operatorname{Syl}_{p}(G)$ and $H$ is a subgroup of $G$ containing $P$ then $P \in \operatorname{Syl}_{p}(H)$.
lean:
theorem Dummit_Foote_exercise_4_5_1a {p : ℕ} {G : Type*} [Group G]
  {P : Sylow p G} (H : Subgroup G) (hH : P ≤ H) :
  IsPGroup p (P.subgroupOf H) ∧
  ∀ (Q : Subgroup H), IsPGroup p Q → (P.subgroupOf H) ≤ Q → Q = (P.subgroupOf H)
reasoning: The informal claim is "P is a Sylow p-subgroup of H." In Mathlib, `Sylow p G` is defined as a maximal p-subgroup. The Lean conclusion unfolds exactly this definition for `P.subgroupOf H`: it asserts (1) `P.subgroupOf H` is a p-group, and (2) any p-subgroup Q of H containing it equals it (maximality). The hypothesis `P ≤ H` correctly captures "H contains P" via the coercion of a Sylow subgroup to a subgroup. The quantifiers and types (arbitrary group G, p : ℕ) match the informal statement. Hence the Lean statement is logically equivalent to the informal claim.
